questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
A time traveler from the eighteenth century or even from 1950 would be amazed at how many goods and services the modern Indian economy delivers and at
how many people can afford them. Compared with any past India has a relatively high standard of living. So our economy must be doing something right, and the time-traveler might want to compliment the person in charge. But who is in charge of the economy?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● As per the Economic Survey 2019-20, wealth creation happens in an economy when the right policy choices are pursued. Wealth creation and economic development in several advanced economies has been guided by **Adam Smith’s philosophy of the invisible hand.**
o The **invisible hand refers** to the way in which the individual pursuit of self-interest can lead to good results for society as a whole. In 1776, in a famous passage in his **book** “**The Wealth of** **Nations”**, the pioneering Scottish economist **Adam Smith** wrote about how individuals, in pursuing their own interests, often end up serving the interests of society as a whole. Of a businessman whose pursuit of profit makes the nation wealthier.
● The evidence since **1991 LPG reforms in India** shows that enabling the invisible hand of markets, i.e., increasing economic openness, has a huge impact in enhancing wealth both in the aggregate and comparative terms.
● **The Economic Survey** states that, “The invisible hand needs to be strengthened by promoting probusiness policies to (i) provide equal opportunities for new entrants, enable fair competition and ease doing business, (ii) eliminate policies that unnecessarily undermine markets through government intervention, (iii) enable trade for job creation, and (iv) efficiently scale up the banking sector to be proportionate to the size of the Indian economy.” **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText: The short-run alternation between economic downturns and upturns is known as:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **business cycle** is the recurring pattern of economic expansion and contraction in an economy, measured by fluctuations in real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) around its long-term growth trend.
o **The four main phases are recovery**, where activity begins to rise from a trough; **expansion**, where the economy grows rapidly; **peak**, the highest point before a slowdown; and **contraction**, or **recession**, when economic activity declines. These cycles are not perfectly regular and are influenced by various economic shocks. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Depression:** It is a major downturn in the business cycle characterized by sharp and sustained declines in economic activity, high rates of unemployment and poverty.
● **Recession**: When the GDP contracts from one quarter to another, the economy is said to be in a recessionary phase. When a recessionary phase sustains for long enough, it is called a recession. In other words, when the **GDP contracts** for a **long period**, the economy is said to be in a recession.
o Economists often consider a **recession** to be in progress **when real GDP has**
**declined for at least two consecutive quarters**.
● **Inflation** is the sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over a period of time, which results in a decrease in the purchasing power of money.
o Inflation in India is measured primarily through the Consumer Price Index (CPI)
Combined (Rural + Urban). The CPI is published monthly by the National Statistical Office (NSO).
**Relevance**: Recently RBI warned that India's growth prospects may be impacted adversely by the risk of prolonged recession due to trade wars.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText: Which one of the following categories of persons is **not** included as a worker/employed person in the Periodic Labour Force Survey?

options.a:
People who are capable of working but have
given up looking for jobs
options.b:
Persons attached to an economic activity but
abstained from work
options.c:
Unpaid household members who assisted in the
household farm
options.d:
People who were seeking or available for work
but could not find a job

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS)** was launched in 2017, conducted by the National Statistics Office (NSO), MoSPI and has been the primary source of statistics on labour force, activity participation of the population and structure of employment and unemployment in the country.
o PLFS aims to estimate the key employment and unemployment indicators, viz., Labour Force Participation Rate (LFPR), Worker Population Ratio (WPR) and Unemployment Rate (UR) on a monthly basis for rural and urban areas as per the current weekly status (CWS) at all-India level.
● **India’s PLFS bases itself on the standards recommended by the 13th International Conference of** **Labour Statisticians (ICLS).** ICLS is a vehicle for standard-setting in labour statistics by the International Labour Organisation (ILO).
o As per the 13th ICLS, “**Employment comprises all persons of working age who**, during a specified brief period, such as one week or one day, were in the following categories: a) **paid** **employment, whether at work or having a job but not at work**; or b) self-employment, whether at work or with an enterprise but not at work.
● **PLFS of India defines employed persons** as persons **who are engaged in any economic activity** or w**ho, despite their attachment to economic activity, abstained themselves from work for reasons of**
**illness, injury or other physical disability**, bad weather, festivals, social or religious functions or other contingencies necessitating temporary absence from work, constituted workers.
o **Unpaid household members who assisted in** **the operation of an economic activity in the** **household farm or non-farm activities** are also considered as workers (as employed).
● As per the PLFS, **persons are classified as not in the** **Labour Force** if, during the specified reference period (a year for Usual Status or a week for CWS), t**hey were** **neither working (employed) nor seeking or available** **for work (unemployed).**
o A **discouraged worker** is a person who is not actively seeking employment but would prefer to work, often because they have given up after long-term unemployment. **These individuals are** **not counted in the official unemployment rate** because they have not searched for a job in the past four weeks, but they are a subset of people marginally attached to the labor force. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Other Key definitions under PLFS**
● **Labour Force Participation Rate (LFPR)**: LFPR is defined as the percentage of persons in the labour force (i.e. working or seeking or available for work) in the population.
● **Worker Population Ratio (WPR)**: WPR is defined as the percentage of employed persons in the population.
● **Unemployment Rate (UR)**: UR is defined as the percentage of persons unemployed among the persons in the labour force.
● **Activity Status:** The activity status of a person is determined based on the activities pursued by the person during the specified reference period.
o **Usual Status:** When the activity status is determined based on the reference period of the last 365 days preceding the date of survey, it is known as the usual activity status of the person. o **Current Weekly Status (CWS)**: The activity status determined based on a reference period of last 7 days preceding the date of survey is known as the CWS of the person.
**Relevance**: Unemployment among the female youth rose for the third time in four months in August, according to data from the statistics ministry’s latest Periodic Labour Force Survey.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The economy’s level of output and its unemployment rate move in opposite directions.
**Statement II:** Aggregate output normally falls in recessions but rises during expansions. **Statement III:** Aggregate output depends on the amount of labor used in the production process.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct
and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct,
but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Unemployment is countercyclical**, meaning it rises when economic growth is low and vice versa. When economic output is high, it signals growth and leads to lower unemployment as firms hire more workers, while low output (a recession) results in higher unemployment as companies reduce their workforce.
o The sensitivity of the unemployment rate to economic growth depends on several factors, most notably on labor market conditions and regulations.
o **Okun’s law** gives the statistical relationship between a country s unemployment rate and the growth rate of its economy. Okun’s law says that **a country’s gross domestic product (GDP)** **must grow at about a 4% rate in one year to achieve a 1% reduction in the rate of** **unemployment,** and a o**ne percentage point increase in unemployment causes a 2% fall in** **GDP. So, statement I is correct.**
● **Aggregate output** **(GDP)** is the economy’s total production of final goods and services for a given time period. **A recession is defined by a fall in aggregate output**, and an **expansion is a period of rising** **aggregate output**. When output falls (recession), firms lay off workers, causing unemployment to rise. When output rises (expansion), firms hire more workers, causing unemployment to fall. Therefore, the cyclical fluctuation of output causes the cyclical fluctuation of the unemployment rate. **So, statement II** **is correct.**
● **Aggregate output depends on the amount of labor used in the production process**, so there is a positive relationship between output and employment. Total employment equals the labor force minus the unemployed, so there is a negative relationship between output and unemployment. Therefore, an increase in the labour employed leads to an increase in aggregate output, which causes a fall in the unemployment rate. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, both Statement–II and Statement–III are correct, and both explain Statement–I.**
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: According to the Monthly bulletin of PLFS India's unemployment rate rose to 5.2% in September 2025, driven by rural job losses.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Efficient functioning of the price mechanism
II. 	 Reduction in the inequalities of incomes
III. 	Consumers' choices guide production
IV. 	 Right to acquire private property
Which of the above are the features of a free enterprise economy?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **A free enterprise** economy can be described as **an economic system in which prices, products, and** **services are determined by market forces rather than government intervention.** In such a system, businesses operate independently with minimal regulatory control. A free enterprise legal system tends to result in capitalism. **It is also referred to as a free market**.
o A free enterprise aims to increase freedom, market efficiency, consumer rights, and economic opportunities.
● **Features** of a **free enterprise** economy include: o **Efficient functioning of the price mechanism**: The price mechanism (supply and demand) is the core mechanism that guides resource allocation. Prices serve as signals and incentives that ensure production is efficient. **So, point I is correct.** o **Consumer Sovereignty**: Producers aim to maximize profit by producing what consumers demand, making consumers the ultimate arbiters of what is produced. **So, point III is correct.** o **Right to acquire private property:** People have the right to acquire private property under free enterprise principles. The ability to own assets and pass them to one's heirs encourages capital accumulation and investment. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Free markets reward factors of production based on their scarcity and productivity**, which naturally leads to significant income and wealth inequality. **Reducing** this **inequality requires government** **intervention** (like progressive taxes), which is typical of a mixed economy (like India), not a free enterprise system. Economic equality is a typical feature of the Communist or Socialist economies, not Capitalist economies. **So, point II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Command economy or centrally planned economy**
● The alternative to a market economy is a command economy, in which there is a central authority making decisions about production and consumption.
o Command economies have been tried, most notably in the Soviet Union
between 1917 and 1991. But they didn’t work very well.
● Its prominent features are: o The government often operates as a monopoly in most key industries,
removing the incentive for efficiency and innovation that comes from competition. o Consumers have very little to no say in the types or quality of goods produced. o The government determines how the final output will be distributed among
citizens, often prioritizing equity or specific national goals over individual wealth.
**Relevance**: Recently, the Competition Commission of India (CCI) organised the 10th National Conference on Economics of Competition Law in New Delhi.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the National Statistical Office, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Central Statistical Organisation and the
National Sample Survey Office  were merged with the National Statistical Office.
II. 	 The Index of Industrial Production and
Consumer Price Indices for Urban/Rural/ Combined are carried out by the NSO.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
With reference to the National Statistical Office, consider the following statements:
I. The Central Statistical Organisation and the National Sample Survey Office  were merged with the National Statistical Office.
II. The Index of Industrial Production and Consumer Price Indices for Urban/Rural/Combined are carried out by the NSO.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct? a) I only b) II only c) Both I and II d) Neither I nor II
**Answer: c**
**Explanation:**
● The **Central Statistical Organisation (CSO) and the National Sample Survey Office(NSSO) were** **merged in May 2019 to form the National Statistical Office (NSO)** under the Ministry of Statistics and
Programme Implementation (MoSPI), aiming to streamline India's statistical system for better data collection, processing, and transparency. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The activities of NSO inter alia include compilation of National Accounts, **Index of Industrial** **Production, Consumer Price Indices for Urban/Rural/Combined,** Human Development Statistics including Gender Statistics and Economic Census and imparting training in Official Statistics. NSO conducts National Sample Survey (NSS) activities through a subordinate office viz. Field Operations Division (FOD). NSO also assists in development of statistics in the States and Union Territories and disseminates Energy Statistics, Social and Environmental Statistics and prepares the National Industrial Classification. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**National Statistics Day:**
● In 2007, the Government of India designated **June 29th** as National Statistics Day to honour Mahalanobis’ birth anniversary.
o Mahalanobis had played a pivotal role in the establishment of the Indian
Statistical Institute (ISI) in 1931 and laid the foundations of the Indian statistical system through the National Sample Survey Office (NSSO) and the Central Statistical Organisation (CSO). At present NSSO and CSO is merged to form o He made pioneering studies in anthropometry in India.
● **This year** it is observed **under the theme “75 Years of National Sample Survey”,** marking the significant contribution of the National Sample Survey in providing timely and reliable statistical data that supports evidence-based decision-making and governance in India.
**Relevance:** As part of the year-long celebrations 75th anniversary of the NSS started from October 2024, NSO's South Zone office in Bengaluru, is organising a celebratory event at Yuvaraja's College, University of Mysuru (one of the pioneering universities which introduced Statistics at graduation level in the country), on September 12, 2025.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Changes to inventories
II. 	 Intermediate goods and services
III. 	Investments
IV. 	 Used goods
V. 	 Financial assets
How many of the above are included in the calculation of GDP?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Gross Domestic Product (GDP)** measures the market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period.
● **Investment** is defined as addition to the stock of physical capital (such as machines, buildings, roads etc., i.e. anything that adds to the future productive capacity of the economy) and changes in the inventory (or the stock of finished goods) of a producer. The stock of unsold finished goods, or semi-finished goods, or raw materials which a firm carries from one year to the next is called **inventory**.
o **Investment spending** and **changes to inventories are included in GDP calculation**. Since spending on investment goods and construction of structures is not directly tied to current output, economists consider such spending to be spending on final goods. And **spending on changes to** **inventories, considered a part of investment spending**, is also included in GDP. Because additional inventory is an investment in future sales. **So, point I and III are correct.**
● **GDP calculation excludes** goods and services that are produced as **inputs** to produce other goods (inputs are often called **intermediate goods**). For example, Steel manufactured for the purpose of producing a car is not counted in aggregate output, but the car is. **So, point II is not correct.**
o **Used goods** are not included in GDP because, as with inputs, to include them would be to doublecount: counting them once when sold as new and again when resold as used. **So, point IV is not** **correct.** o **Financial assets such as stocks and bonds are not included in GDP** because they don’t represent either the production or the sale of final goods and services. **So, point V is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Methods of GDP calculation**
1. **The Product or Value-Added Method**: This method adds up the value of all the final goods and services produced in the economy—a calculation that excludes the value of intermediate goods and services.
2. **Expenditure Method**: It calculates GDP by summing up all the spending on **final goods and** **services** by the four main sectors of the economy: households, businesses, government, and the foreign sector.
**.** **GDP = C + I + G + (X - M).** . Where, **C (Consumption)** = Spending by households on final goods and services (e.g., food, rent, cars, haircuts). . **I (Investment)** = Spending by businesses on capital goods plus changes in inventories. . **G (Government Spending)** = Spending by the government on final goods and services (e.g., military, infrastructure, public worker salaries). Excludes transfer payments (like welfare, subsidies). . **(X - M) (Net Exports)** = Exports (X) minus Imports (M).
2. **Income Method**: This method calculates GDP by summing up all the income earned by the
factors of production (land, labor, capital, and entrepreneurship) used to produce the final goods and services within the country. **GDP** = Wages + Rent + Interest + Profit + (Taxes on Production -Subsidies) + Depreciation

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Household savings
II.	 Balance of payments
III. 	Government budget deficit
IV. 	 Investment by industries
Which of the above can be calculated from the 'circular flow of income'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **circular flow of income** connects the four sectors of the economy—households, firms, government, and the rest of the world—via three types of markets: the factor markets, the markets for goods and services, and the financial markets. Funds flow from firms to households in the form of wages, profit, interest, and rent through the factor markets.
● **Households** normally don’t spend all their disposable income on goods and services. Instead, part of their income is typically set aside as **private savings**, which **goes into financial markets**. This is the flow of unconsumed income from households to the financial market. It is a fundamental measurable flow that leaves the inner consumption loop. **So, point I is correct.**
● The balance of payments (BOP) cannot be directly calculated from the basic circular flow of income model which is a model in closed economies, because the standard model focuses on domestic flows. However, **an open economy's expanded circular flow model incorporates the foreign sector, making** **it possible to track the flows needed for BOP** calculation by adding exports (credits) and imports (debits), as well as the financial flows included in the capital and financial accounts. **So, point II is** **correct.**
● **The Government Budget Deficit** is the difference between the Injection (Government Spending, G) and the Leakage (Taxes, T) within the government sector. The government returns part of the money it collects from taxes to households in the form of government transfers.
o The amount that the government collects in taxes does not need to equal the amount that it pays out for government purchases and transfers. If the government spends more than it gathers in taxes, then it must borrow from the financial markets to make up the shortfall. Thus, the Government Budget Deficit is counted in the circular flow of income model, therefore it can be calculated from the **'circular flow of income' model. So, point III is correct.**
● **Investment by Industries is a major** **injection of funds from the financial** **market back into the production sector** **(Firms).** Via the financial markets, private savings and funds from the rest of the world are channeled **into investment spending by** **firms**, government borrowing, foreign borrowing and lending, and foreign transactions of stocks. Thus, it can be **calculated** from the **'circular flow of** **income' model.** **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: India's household savings continued their downward trajectory for the third straight year, slipping to 18.1 per cent of GDP in financial year 2024 (FY24), as per CareEdge Ratings.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 GVA at basic prices = GVA at factor costs +
Net production taxes
II. 	 GVA at market prices = GVA at basic prices +
Net product taxes
III. 	Net National Product = GDP – Depreciation
IV. 	 National Income = NNP at market prices –
Net indirect taxes
Which of the given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Gross Value Added (GVA)** provides a monetary value for goods and services that have been produced in a country, minus the cost of all inputs, raw materials, and intermediate consumption that are directly attributable to that production. GVA thus adjusts gross domestic product (GDP) by the impact of subsidies and taxes (tariffs) on products.
o **GVA at basic prices** is defined as output valued at basic prices less intermediate consumption valued at purchasers’ prices. **It is calculated as:** GVA at Basic Prices = GVA at Factor Cost + Net taxes (Production Taxes - Production Subsidies). **So, point I is correct.** o **GVA at market prices** is the value of output at the price the consumer pays, reflecting the total amount of money realized from the sale of goods and services produced by all sectors of the economy. **It is calculated as:** GVA at Market Prices = GVA at Basic Prices + Net Product Taxes. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Net National Product (NNP)** represents the total market value of all final goods and services produced by a nation's residents in a given period, after accounting for the value lost through the consumption of capital.
o **It is calculated as: NNP = GNP – Depreciation**. **So, point III is not correct.** o **GNP** is the total market value of all final goods and services produced by the residents of a country during a specific period (usually a year), computed before allowance is made for the depreciation of capital. o **GNP = GDP + Net factor income from abroad**.
o **Depreciation**, also known as Consumption of Fixed Capital (CFC) in national income accounting, is the economic concept that measures the loss in the value of fixed capital assets (machinery, buildings, equipment, etc.) over time.
● **National income** is the total amount of income earned by the factors of production (land, labor, capital, and entrepreneurship) belonging to the normal residents of a country in a given financial year. It is equal to NNP at factor cost.
o **Thus, NNP at factor cost = National Income (NI)** o **National Income** = NNP at market prices – Net indirect taxes (Net indirect taxes = Indirect taxes – Subsidies). **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Cash in the hands of the public
II. 	 Checkable bank deposits
III.	 Debit cards
IV. 	 Certificate of deposits
V. 	 Deposit of banks with other banks
Which of the above forms part of the money supply in the economy?

options.a: I, II, III and IV only
options.c: II, III, IV and V only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The money supply means the total stock of monetary media of exchange available to a society for use in connection with the economic activity of the country. **The main measures of money supply are M1,** **M2, M3, and M4**, which classify money based on its liquidity and accessibility.
● **M1** includes the most liquid assets, such as physical currency and demand deposits. o **M2** includes M1 plus less liquid assets like savings accounts and money market funds. o **M3 is often called "broad money"** and adds time deposits to M1, while **M4** adds post office savings deposits to M3.
● Cash in the hands of the public: Currency with the public is the most liquid money. It is part of M1 or Narrow Money.  **So, point I is correct.**
● **Checkable Bank Deposits: Demand deposits** of the public with the banks is the other important component of money supply. **These demand deposits held by the public are also called bank money** **or deposit money.**
o Deposits with the banks are broadly divided into two types: demand deposits and time deposits. **Demand deposits** in the banks are those deposits which can be withdrawn by drawing cheques on them. Through cheques, these deposits can be transferred to others for making payments from whom goods and services have been purchased. Thus, cheques make these demand deposits as a medium of exchange and therefore make them to serve as money. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Debit cards (bank ATMs):** Debit cards are a type of payment card that allows consumers to make purchases by directly accessing the funds in their checking or savings accounts. They function as an electronic substitute for cash, enabling users to make transactions without the need to carry physical currency.
o **Debit cards are simply alternatives to cheques. They are not different categories of liquid** **assets. Thus, they do not separately affect** measures of the money supply. **So, point III is not** **correct.**
● A **Certificate of Deposit (CD)** is a type of savings account that pays a fixed interest rate on your deposit for an agreed-upon period. **These are Time Deposits (TD) or quasi-money.** They are **included in** **broader money supply measures (M3)** because they are less liquid than demand deposits. **So, point IV** **is correct.**
● **Deposit of banks** **with other banks:** These are Inter-bank deposits, as financial institutions deposit their surplus money with other banks, in return for interest. They are held by financial institutions, not the nonbank public, so t**hey are not counted as part of the money available for public transactions/savings**. **So, point V is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Banking system liquidity hits Rs 3 lakh crore in June 2025, highest in 3 years, driven by RBI’s Rs
8.6 lakh crore infusion and government’s month-end spending.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Commercial banks create credit through the process of fractional reserve banking.
**Statement II:** Commercial banks must keep all the deposits they receive in the form of highly liquid assets.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct
and Statement-II explains Statement-I
options.b:
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct,
but Statement-II does **not** explain Statement-I
options.c:
Statement-I is correct, but statement-II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement-I is incorrect, but statement-II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A bank is a financial intermediary that uses liquid assets in the form of bank deposits to finance the illiquid investments of borrowers. Banks can create liquidity because it isn’t necessary for a bank to keep all the funds deposited with it in the form of highly liquid assets.
o **Commercial banks create credit through the process of fractional reserve banking (Cash** **Reserve Ratio)**. When a bank receives a deposit, it keeps only a fraction as reserves and lends out the rest. o The loan funds are then deposited back into the banking system, leading to a multiple expansion of the money supply. This process is known as the money multiplier. **So, statement I is correct.**
● If banks were required to keep all the deposits they receive in the form of highly liquid assets (meaning a 100% reserve ratio), they would not be able to lend any money.
o Without lending, no credit creation would be possible, and the money supply would not expand. **Banks are only required to maintain a fraction of their deposits as reserves (CRR and SLR).** The fraction of bank deposits that a bank holds as reserves is its **reserve ratio**. **So, statement II** **is not correct.** **So, Statement I is correct, but Statement II is incorrect.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Full-reserve banking**
● **Full-reserve banking, also known as 100% reserve banking**, refers to a system of banking where banks are not allowed to lend out money that they receive from customers in the form of demand deposits.
o Demand deposits are deposits that customers can withdraw from the bank at any point in time without any prior notice. o So, under full-reserve banking, banks are mandated to hold all money that they receive as demand deposits from customers in their vaults.
● **In this case, banks simply act as custodians to depositors’ money and may charge a** **fee from depositors for the service of safekeeping** that they offer to the depositors.
● Under a full-reserve banking system, banks can only lend money that they receive as time deposits from their customers.
o Time deposits are deposits that customers can withdraw from the bank only after a certain period of time that is agreed upon between the bank and its customers.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are functions of money in
an economy?
I. 	 Unit of account
II. 	 Holding purchasing power over time
III. 	Standard of deferred payments
IV. 	 Creation of wealth
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● Money is defined in terms of what it does: money is any asset that can easily be used to purchase goods and services. Money consists either of cash itself, which is liquid by definition, or of other assets that are highly liquid.
o **Money plays three main roles in any modern economy: it is a medium of exchange, a store** **of value, and a unit of account**.
● **Money normally serves as a unit of account**—a measure individuals use to set prices and make economic calculations. **So, point I is correct.**
● To act as a medium of exchange, **money must also be a store of value**—a means of holding purchasing power over time. Any asset that holds its purchasing power over time is a store of value. So, the store-ofvalue role is necessary but not distinctive. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Role of money as a medium of exchange**—an asset that individuals use to trade for goods and services rather than for consumption. **Money has always been used as a standard of deferred payment.** As a result of this function, it has become possible to express future payments in terms of money. A borrower who borrows a certain sum in the present undertakes to pay the same in future. **For example, buying** **books in cash.** **So, point III is correct.**
● **Money is a medium for measuring and transferring wealth**, but **it is not the source of wealth itself.** **Wealth is created through the production of goods and services**. Wealth generation primarily happens through the process of production, value creation, and capital accumulation. **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Type of Money**
● **Commodity money** is a good used as a medium of exchange that has other uses.
● **A commodity-backed money** is a medium of exchange with no intrinsic value whose ultimate value is guaranteed by a promise that it can be converted into valuable goods.
● **Fiat money** is a medium of exchange whose value derives entirely from its official status as a means of payment. For example, modern-day currency.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to bank deposit insurance in India,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is guaranteed by the Reserve Bank of India.
II. 	 Deposits in primary cooperative societies are
not insured.
III. 	Multiple accounts of an individual in the
same bank are insured separately.
IV. 	 The premium for deposit insurance is borne
by the insured bank.
Which of the statements given above are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● Deposit insurance is guaranteed by the **Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation** **(DICGC)**. While the DICGC is a wholly owned subsidiary of the RBI, **the legal guarantee is provided** **by the DICGC.**
o The functions of the DICGC are governed by the provisions of 'The **Deposit Insurance and** **Credit Guarantee Corporation Act, 1961' (DICGC Act)** and 'The Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation General Regulations, 1961' framed by the Reserve Bank of India in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (3) of Section 50 of the said Act. **So, statement I** **is not correct.**
● **The DICGC insures all deposits such as savings, fixed, current, recurring, etc. deposits**. The DICGC does not provide insurance for deposits by foreign, central, and state governments, and for inter-bank deposits.
o It **insures deposits with all commercial banks,** including branches of foreign banks functioning in India, local area banks and regional rural banks.
o At present, all co-operative banks are covered by the DICGC. **However, Primary cooperative** **societies are not insured by the DICGC. So, statement II is correct.**
● **Deposits held by an individual in the same bank** and in the same right and same capacity (e.g., all savings, fixed, and current accounts under their own name) **are aggregated (clubbed together)**. The **maximum insurance cover of ₹5 lakh** (principal + interest) applies to this total aggregated amount per bank. **So, statement III is not correct.**
o However, in case of deposits with more than one bank, the deposit insurance coverage limit is applied separately to the deposits in each bank. o Accounts held in different capacities (Current account of Firms and joint account) are insured separately.
● The **premium for deposit insurance is borne by the insured bank.** DICGC collects premiums from member financial institutions, and at present, it is charging 12 paise/Rs 100 cap on deposit insurance premiums. **So, statement IV is correct.**
o The premium to be paid by the insured banks are computed based on their assessable deposits. o The premium which was previously collected quarterly from the insured bank,s was made payable half yearly from 1979. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Subsidiaries of RBI:**
● **Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation (DICGC):** Established in 1978 (formed from the merger of the Deposit Insurance Corporation and the Credit Guarantee Corporation of India), the DICGC's core function is to provide deposit insurance to bank customers, guaranteeing deposits up to a limit (currently ₹5 lakh per depositor, per bank).
o It is governed by the Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation Act, 1961.
● **Bharatiya Reserve Bank Note Mudran Private Limited (BRBNMPL)**: Incorporated in 1995, this company's core function is to manage and operate the currency printing presses for the production and supply of currency notes across India.
● **Reserve Bank Information Technology Pvt. Ltd. (ReBIT):** Incorporated in 2016, ReBIT's core function is to provide the Information Technology (IT) and cybersecurity solutions and services necessary for the RBI's internal functioning and for the financial sector.
o **It is governed by the Companies Act, 2013.**
● **Indian Financial Technology and Allied Services (IFTAS):** Incorporated in 2016, IFTAS's core function is to design, develop, and deploy specialized technology-related services and infrastructure (like INFINET) for the financial sector, assisting banks and other financial institutions with their technological requirements. It is also governed by the Companies Act, 2013.
● The **Reserve Bank Innovation Hub (RBIH)** is a wholly owned subsidiary of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and was set up to solve India's intricate problems in the financial sector.
o It has been set up as a Section 8 company under the Companies Act, 2013 with an initial capital contribution of Rs. 100 crores.
**Relevance**: The Union Government is actively considering a proposal to raise the deposit insurance limit beyond ₹5 lakh to 10 lakh.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The economy is considered to be self-correcting in the long run.
**Statement II:** During a recessionary gap, lower wages can cause production to rise. **Statement III:** During an inflationary gap, there is a rise in nominal wages.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct and both of them explain Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct, but only one of them explains Statement–I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements–II and III is
correct and that explains Statement–I
options.d:
Neither Statement–II nor Statement–III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **In the long run the economy is self-correcting** because shocks to aggregate demand affect aggregate output in the short run but not the long run. In the long-run perspective, all factors of production such as labour, capital, and technology are considered flexible, and the economy is expected to adjust naturally to shocks or imbalances. **So, statement I is correct.**
● During a **recessionary gap**, aggregate output is below potential output. The surplus of labor reduces workers' bargaining power, causing nominal wages to eventually fall. However, **lower wages reduce** **production costs for firms, causing the short-run aggregate supply curve to shift right, restoring** **equilibrium**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● During an **inflationary gap**, aggregate output is above potential output. An inflationary gap means unemployment is low (labor shortage). The scarcity of labor increases workers' bargaining power, **causing** **nominal wages to eventually rise.** **So, statement III is correct.**
o Higher wages increase production costs for firms, causing the short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) curve to shift left, restoring equilibrium. Thus, there is a movement of nominal wages in both directions which is a necessary condition for the principle of long-run self-correction to hold true. **So, both Statement–II and Statement–III are correct and both of them explain Statement–I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: While year-on-year inflation fell from 3.2% in April to 2.8% in May 2025, the Periodic Labour Force Survey shows that the unemployment rate rose from 5.1% in April to 5.8% in May.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Local Area Bank
II. 	 National Bank for Agriculture and Rural
Development
III. 	Lead Bank Scheme
IV. 	 Regional Rural Bank
Arrange the above institutions/schemes in chronological order of their introduction:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● The genesis of the **Lead Bank Scheme (LBS)** can be traced to the Study Group headed by Prof. **D. R.** **Gadgil (Gadgil Study Group)** on the Organisational Framework for the Implementation of the Social Objectives, which submitted its report in **October 1969**.
o A Committee of Bankers on Branch Expansion Programme of public sector banks appointed by the RBI under the Chairmanship of Shri F. K. F. Nariman **(Nariman Committee)** endorsed the idea of an  ‘**Area Approach**’ in its report (November 1969), recommending that in order to enable the Public Sector Banks to discharge their social responsibilities, each bank should concentrate on certain districts where it should act as a 'Lead Bank'.
● **Initially 5 Regional Rural Banks** (**RRBs) were established in the year 1975**; the first RRB established in India was the **Prathama Grameen Bank**.
o It was set up on October 2, 1975, in Moradabad, Uttar Pradesh under the provisions of the Ordinance promulgated on 26th September 1975, with an objective to create an alternative channel to the cooperative credit structure to facilitate an expanded bandwidth of institutional credit for agriculture and rural sector. o They were institutionalized under the Regional Rural Banks RRBs Act, 1976. o **RRBs have a three-tiered ownership structure**: 50% Central Government, 35% Sponsor Bank, and 15% State Government. o They are managed by a Board of Directors and are regulated by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and supervised by NABARD.
● **National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD)** was established on 12 July **1982** by an Act of Parliament. **NABARD is wholly owned by the Government of India.**
o **NABARD, as a Development Bank** with mandate of major functions of NABARD include promotion and development, refinancing, financing, planning, monitoring and supervision.
● **Local Area Banks (LABS)** as an idea was conceived in **1996**. The LABs were expected to focus on the local customers, adhere to the priority sector lending targets as applicable to other domestic banks, including the targets on loans to weaker sections. The Scheme envisaged Local Area Banks with a minimum capital of Rs.. 5 crore and an area of operation comprising three contiguous districts. Although six local banks were licensed by RBI, at present only four Local Area Banks are functioning.
o **For example**, **Krishna Bhima Samruddhi Local Area Bank** was established in 1999 for the regions of Krishna and Bhima in Andhra Pradesh and Telangana.
● **So, the correct chronological order would be**: o **Lead Bank Scheme (1969) (III)** o **Regional Rural Banks (1975) (IV)** o **National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD) 1982 (II)** o **Local Area Banks (1996) (I)** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Loans are lot less liquid than bonds.
**Statement II:** Unlike bonds, loans are easy to resell as standardized products.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I.
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I.
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but Statement II is
incorrect.
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is
correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**: An asset is liquid if it can be quickly converted into cash, without much loss of value, and it is illiquid if it cannot.
● **A loan is a direct, bilateral contract, meaning it's tailored to the specific borrower and lender.** It is often difficult and time-consuming to resell because it is not a standardized product like bonds. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **A bond is a promise by the seller to pay interest each year and to repay the principal to the owner** **of the bond on a particular date.** So, a bond is a financial asset from its owner’s point of view and a liability from its issuer’s point of view. **Bonds can be easily sold on the bond market as a standardized** **product**—a product with clearly defined terms and quality.
● An important **advantage of bonds, compared to loans, is that they are easy to resell.** This provides liquidity to bond purchasers. Indeed, a bond will often pass through many hands before it finally comes due. **Loans, in contrast, are much more difficult to resell** because, unlike bonds, they are not standardized: they differ in size, quality, and terms. **This makes them a lot less liquid than bonds. So,** **statement II is not correct.** **So, Statement I is correct, but Statement II is incorrect.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Despite the cut in repo rate by RBI, India’s 10-year benchmark government bond yield has risen by about 26 basis points.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the origin of the institution
of Banking in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Bank of Hindustan was the first bank in
India set up by the British.
II. 	 The Allahabad Bank was the first Indian
owned bank.
III. 	‘Anyonya Sahakari Mandali’ was the first
cooperative credit society in India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● Modern banking in India originated in the last decades of the 18th century. The **"Bank of Hindustan"** was the first bank **established in India in 1770**, founded by the agency house Alexander and Company in Calcutta. It ultimately failed and was closed in 1832. **So, statement I is correct.**
o The **General Bank of India (1786)** was the second. Both were set up by European agency houses. Thereafter, three presidency banks namely the Bank of Bengal (this bank was originally started in the year 1806 as Bank of Calcutta and then in the year 1809 became the Bank of Bengal), the Bank of Bombay and the Bank of Madras, were set up.
● **Allahabad Bank (1865)** was the **oldest surviving Joint Stock bank** (founded by Europeans). It was nationalized by the government in 1969 and merged with the Indian Bank in 2020.
o The **first commercial bank started with entirely Indian capital and management was** the **Punjab National Bank (PNB) in 1895**. It was jointly established by Dyal Singh Majithia, Lala
Harkishen Lal and Lala Lajpat Rai. The bank was registered in 1894, and commenced business in 1895, in Lahore. o The **Oudh Commercial Bank, 1881**, was the first commercial bank with limited liability and partial Indian management. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The first known mutual aid society in India was the **‘Anyonya Sahakari Mandali’ organised in the** **erstwhile princely State of Baroda in 1889** under the guidance of **Vithal Laxman also known as** **Bhausaheb Kavthekar**. It was the earliest known formal cooperative credit association in India. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** A contractionary fiscal policy is used to close an inflationary gap.
**Statement II:** A contractionary fiscal policy shifts the aggregate demand curve leftwards.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● An **inflationary gap** occurs when the economy is in a state of excessive demand, operating beyond its full employment potential, which leads to rising prices. The appropriate macroeconomic response is a contractionary policy designed to reduce the excessive Aggregate Demand to close an inflationary gap. **So, statement I is correct.**
● A **contractionary policy is a monetary measure to reduce government spending or the rate of** **monetary expansion by a central bank.** It is a macroeconomic tool used to combat rising inflation. Contractionary fiscal policy works by reducing the components of aggregate expenditure.
o Decreased government expenditure directly lowers aggregate spending and Increased tax rates lower disposable income, forcing households to reduce consumption (C) and investment (I), which further lowers aggregate spending. o Overall, it causes a decrease in aggregate spending across the economy. A decrease in aggregate spending is represented by a leftward shift of the Aggregate Demand (AD) curve. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Inflation cooled to 1.54 percent in September 2025, the lowest reading in more than eight years, easing from a two-month high of 2.07 percent in August 2025.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
The term 'cyclically adjusted budget balance' is an
estimate of what the government's budget balance would be if:

options.a:
Nominal Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is
exactly equal to Real GDP
options.b: Real GDP is exactly equal to potential output
options.c:
Fiscal deficit is negligible due to high tax
compliance
options.d:
Potential output is significantly more than the
budget estimate

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**: To separate the effect of the business cycle from the effects of other factors, many governments produce an estimate of what the budget balance would be if there were neither a recessionary nor an inflationary gap.
● The **cyclically adjusted budget balance** is an estimate of what the budget balance would be **if real GDP** **were exactly equal to potential output.** It takes into account the extra tax revenue the government would collect and the transfers it would save if a recessionary gap were eliminated—or the revenue the government would lose and the extra transfers it would make if an inflationary gap were eliminated. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Economic Term
Description
I. - Haircut The difference between the value of a loan and the value of the collateral pledged
II. - Implicit liabilities
Future government obligations that are not included in official debt statistics
III. - Opportunity cost
The cost of the next best alternative forgone when a choice is made
IV. - Insider trading
When stockholders of the same company transfer trade shares amongst themselves
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● In banking and finance, a **"haircut"** is a risk-management measure referring to a reduction or discount applied to the market value of an asset that is used as collateral for a loan (**The difference between the** **value of a loan and the value of the collateral pledged**)**.** This reduction provides a safety cushion for the lender against potential losses if the value of the collateral drops or if the borrower defaults. The lender only permits a borrower to use the discounted value of the asset for borrowing purposes.
o **For example,** if a borrower pledges government bonds worth ₹10 lakh as collateral, and the bank applies a 10% haircut, the bank will consider the collateral's value to be only ₹9 lakh, thus sanctioning a loan of a maximum of ₹9 lakh. The ₹1 lakh difference serves as a buffer to protect the bank in case the bond's market value declines before it can be sold to recover the loan amount in the event of default. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Implicit liabilities** are vast, **long-term government spending promises** (e.g., future social security and national healthcare payments) that represent a fiscal burden but aren't included in official debt statistics. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Opportunity cost** represents **the desirable benefits someone foregoes by choosing one alternative** **instead of another**. To properly evaluate opportunity cost, the costs and benefits of every option available must be considered and weighed against the others. Considering potential opportunity costs can guide individuals and organisations to more profitable decision-making. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Insider trading** is when **a breach of a fiduciary duty** or other relationship of trust and confidence **happens in trading a security (for example, a stock) based on nonpublic information that only a few** **people are privy to.** The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) also issued its Prohibition of Insider Trading (Amendment) Regulations, 2025, in March. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only three of the four pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: SEBI issued an order recently against two officials of the country's federal power regulator and officials of GNA Energy, alleging insider trading of Indian Energy Exchange (IEX).

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Non-Performing Assets (NPA):
I. 	 A loan loss provision is an expense on the
income statement set aside to cover potential uncollectible loans.
II. 	 Lenders can also convert bad loans into equity
or stock shares, which may appreciate in value and cover the losses from the defaulted loan.
III. 	Gross NPAs of public sector banks have been
declining during the last five financial years.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
A non-performing asset (NPA) is a loan or advance for which the borrower has stopped making payments for more than 90 days. This means the asset is no longer generating income for the lender, indicating a high risk of default.
● **Loan loss provisions**, which are set aside to cover potential losses, reduce the capital available to provide subsequent loans. Once the actual losses from defaulted loans are determined, they are written off against earnings.
○ A loan loss provision is a financial safeguard used by banks to cover losses from unpaid loans or loan defaults. **Banks allocate these expenses on their income statement to ensure accurate** **reporting of their financial status and manage risks associated with lending.** By adding to their loan loss reserves, banks prepare for potential defaults, thereby giving a precise picture of their overall financial health. **So, statement I is correct.**
● If defaulted loans are secured by assets, lenders can repossess and sell the collateral to cover losses, depending on resale value.
○ **Lenders can also convert bad loans into equity or stock shares, which may appreciate in** **value and cover the losses from the defaulted loan.** Companies with bond debt can also convert their debt to equity shares if they can't repay the bond to investors. When bonds get converted to new equity shares, it wipes out the value of the original share. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Gross non-performing assets (NPAs) of public sector banks have been declining during the last five** **financial years.** The NPAs have reduced from 9.11% to 2.58% from March 2021 to March 2025. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Which of the following parameters are used
as indicators for placing a bank under Prompt Corrective Action by the Reserve Bank of India?
I. 	 Capital to Risk-Weighted Assets Ratio
(CRAR)
II. 	 Net Non-Performing Assets (Net NPA) Ratio
III. 	Return on Assets (RoA)
IV. 	 Ratio of a bank’s total assets to its Tier 1
capital
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) framework** is a supervisory mechanism introduced by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) to maintain the financial soundness and stability of banks. It acts as an early warning system that enables the RBI to take timely corrective measures when a bank shows signs of financial stress.
● **The Capital to Risk-Weighted Assets Ratio (CRAR),** also known as the Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR), **measures a bank’s capital in relation to its risk-weighted assets**. It ensures that a bank has sufficient capital to absorb potential losses and continue operations. Under the PCA framework, a breach of prescribed CRAR levels is a key trigger for corrective action. The thresholds for CRAR breaches are clearly defined by the RBI under different risk categories. **So, point I is correct.**
● **The Net NPA ratio indicates the quality of a bank’s loan portfolio** and measures the proportion of nonperforming assets after deducting provisions. A higher Net NPA ratio shows poor asset quality and potential solvency risk. The RBI’s PCA framework includes this parameter to assess the level of stressed assets, as **rising NPAs affect both profitability and capital adequacy.** Breaching the Net NPA threshold may lead to restrictions on credit expansion and management compensation. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **Return on Assets (RoA)** measures the profitability of a bank relative to its total assets. It reflects how efficiently a bank is generating profits from its asset base. **Persistent negative RoA indicates** **sustained losses and weak financial performance**, prompting RBI intervention under the PCA framework. Hence, profitability is an essential parameter for evaluating the operational efficiency and stability of a bank. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Leverage** refers to the ratio of a bank’s total assets to its Tier 1 capital and indicates the extent of debt in its capital structure. **A high leverage ratio signifies that a bank is excessively dependent on borrowed** **funds, thereby increasing its financial vulnerability**. The RBI included leverage as a key parameter in the revised PCA framework (2017) to ensure banks maintain an optimal balance between capital and total assets. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In recent years, the RBI has tightened supervision under the PCA framework, especially following the financial stress in several public sector and cooperative banks.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Penalties for violating traffic laws
II. 	 Government ownership of a bank account
with no legal claimant
III. 	Completion of Initial Public Offering of the
LIC
IV. 	 World Bank's loan to India's National
Tuberculosis Elimination Programme
Which of the above are part of the revenue receipts of the government?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
In the structure of the Union Government’s budget, **revenue receipts are those receipts that do not create any** **liability or lead to a reduction in assets of the government.** They are classified into tax revenue (like income tax, GST, customs duties) and non-tax revenue (like interest receipts, dividends, fines, and grants). In contrast, capital receipts either create liabilities (like borrowings) or reduce assets (like disinvestment).
● **Penalties, fines, and fees** collected by the government for **violating rules — such as traffic laws** — are examples of non-tax revenue. They neither create any liability nor reduce any asset; instead, they represent current income for the government. These collections are accounted for under the “Miscellaneous NonTax Revenue” head in the Union Budget. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Escheat:** When a bank account remains unclaimed for a long period and the owner cannot be traced, the amount is transferred to the Senior Citizens’ Welfare Fund (SCWF) or credited to the Consolidated Fund
of India. **Such transfers are treated as non-tax revenue since they are current receipts** that do not involve asset reduction or liability creation for the government. **So, point II is correct.**
● **The proceeds from the sale of government equity in public sector undertakings (PSUs), such as the** **LIC IPO (2022), constitute disinvestment receipts**. These are part of capital receipts, as they involve the sale of government assets (equity shares). Hence, disinvestment reduces the ownership assets of the government and does not count as a revenue receipt. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Financial support or grants from international institutions like the World Bank for social sector** **programmes are generally treated as non-tax revenue** if they come as grants or aid (not loans). In this case, since it is an external assistance it does create a liability, it falls under the category of capital receipts. **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union Budget 2024–25 emphasised improving the composition of revenue receipts through better tax compliance and enhanced non-tax revenues.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Public Debt Management Cell is an
interim step to eventually take over the government's debt management functions from the Reserve Bank of India.
II. 	 The central government aims to reduce its
outstanding liabilities to around 60% of GDP by March 2031.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Public Debt Management Cell (PDMC) is a non-statutory, advisory body within the Ministry of Finance, while the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is India's statutory central bank and current public debt manager. The PDMC was created as an interim step to eventually take over the government's debt management functions from the RBI. Its functions are strictly advisory to avoid conflict with the RBI's current statutory role.
● **Public Debt Management Cell** is an interim arrangement before setting up an independent and statutory debt management agency, namely the Public Debt Management Agency (PDMA). PDMC has the following advisory functions to the Government.
○ P**lan borrowings of the Government, including market borrowings, other domestic** **borrowings, and SGBs.** ○ **Manage the Central Government’s liabilities, including NSSF, and contingent liabilities.** ○ Monitor cash balances of the Government, improve cash forecasting and promote efficient cash management practices. ○ **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The central government aims to reduce its outstanding liabilities to around 50% of GDP by March** **2031**. In 2025- 26, outstanding liabilities are estimated to be 56.1% of the GDP.  **So, statement II is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union Budget 2024–25 set a new timeline for bringing down public debt.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the borrowing mechanisms
available to the State Governments from the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Ways and Means Advances (WMA)
facility helps State Governments to manage temporary mismatches between receipts and payments.
II. 	 The Reserve Bank of India acts as the debt
manager to the States and conducts auctions of State Developmental Loans on their behalf.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)** plays a pivotal role as the banker to State Governments under the RBI Act, 1934, providing short-term financial accommodation and managing their market borrowings. The borrowing by States from the RBI primarily occurs through two major channels — Ways and Means Advances (WMA) for short-term needs, and State Development Loans (SDLs) for long-term capital financing.
● **The Ways and Means Advances (WMA) facility is a short-term credit mechanism provided by the** **RBI to State Governments to help them manage temporary mismatches between receipts and** **payments**. WMAs are governed by Section 17(5) of the RBI Act, 1934, and are meant strictly for temporary liquidity adjustment, not for financing long-term deficits. There are two types of WMAs — Normal WMA (based on the three-year average of State revenue and capital expenditure) and Special
WMA (secured against the holdings of Government securities). The interest rate on WMA is aligned with the RBI’s repo rate, and when limits are breached, the overdraft facility applies at a higher interest rate. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **State Development Loans (SDLs)** are market borrowings undertaken by the States for long-term funding needs, particularly for **financing developmental and infrastructure projects**. **The RBI acts as the debt** **manager to the States and conducts auctions of SDLs on their behalf under the powers vested by** **the RBI Act, 1934**. SDLs are dated securities similar to Central Government securities (G-Secs) but are issued by individual States. The interest rates (yields) are determined through market-based bidding, and repayment obligations rest with the issuing State Government. SDLs form a major part of the States’ capital receipts and contribute to financing the fiscal deficit while maintaining transparency in borrowing. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Several States in FY 2024–25 have increasingly relied on State Development Loans to finance postpandemic development expenditure, especially in infrastructure, health, and education sectors.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Goods and Services Tax
(GST), consider the following statements:
I. 	 There was a marginal decline in gross GST
collections in the FY 2024–25 over the previous year.
II. 	 The highest tax slab applicable to goods and
services in India stands at 28%.
III. 	The total number of active GST taxpayers in
India is now more than 1.5 crore.
Which of the statements given above are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Goods and Services Tax (GST)** is India’s destination-based indirect tax system, introduced to subsume multiple central and state indirect taxes, thereby simplifying tax compliance and reducing cascading of taxes. The GST regime has been progressively evolving, expanding its tax base and adjusting rate structures to balance revenue mobilisation with taxpayer ease.
● **Official release states that for FY 2024–25, gross GST collections reached ₹22.08 lakh crore,** **representing a 9.4% increase over the previous year’s collection of ₹20.18 lakh crore.** This signifies a robust expansion in tax compliance and consumption, not a decline. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● India’s GST system was structured around multiple slabs—5%, 12%, 18%, and 28%—with additional compensation cess on luxury and sin goods. **However, a** major overhaul of the GST rate structure was approved by the GST Council in September 2025, which introduced a “de-merit” rate of 40 per cent for luxury/sin goods (with standard slabs revised to 5 per cent and 18 per cent). **Hence, the highest slab** **applicable to goods and services under the current structure is 40%, not 28%.** **So, Statement II is** **not correct.**
● **According to the official data for FY 2024–25, the number of active GST taxpayers rose to over 1.51** **crore by March 2025**. This reflects the continued expansion of the tax base due to improved registration compliance and economic formalisation. **So, Statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The record high gross GST collection of ₹22.08 lakh crore for FY 2024-25 reflects increased formalisation of the Indian economy and better compliance mechanisms under GST.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following categories of companies:
I. 	 Foreign companies with income sources in
India
II. 	 Foreign companies that do not have a
permanent establishment in India
III. 	Foreign Institutional Investors
IV. 	 Companies located in Special Economic
Zones
How many of the above pay Minimum Alternate Tax in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The concept of Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) was first introduced as a measure of equity and fairness in the corporate tax system. It is levied as a percentage of a company’s book profit, ensuring that “zero tax companies” also bear a minimum tax burden.
● MAT serves as a corrective measure against the practice of “zero-tax companies” — those that report substantial book profits but pay negligible or no tax because of extensive use of tax incentives, exemptions, and depreciation benefits.
○ Under Section 115JB of the Income Tax Act, if the income tax payable by a company under normal provisions is less than 15% of its book profit (as per the latest rate after amendments), the company must pay MAT at 15% of the book profit plus applicable surcharge and cess. ○ **A foreign company that earns income from business operations in India or from a permanent** **establishment (as per Article 5 of Double Taxation Avoidance Agreements) is liable to pay** **MAT under Section 115JB**, unless specifically exempted. The Supreme Court, in CIT v. Yokogawa India Ltd. (2017), and subsequent amendments clarified that MAT applies to foreign companies only when they have a place of business or PE in India. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Following the 2015 amendment to the Income Tax Act, and subsequent clarification by the Finance** **Act, 2016, MAT provisions were made inapplicable to foreign companies** **that do not have a** **permanent establishment (PE) in India or** that are taxed under special provisions such as capital gains, interest, royalties, or fees for technical services. Therefore, MAT does not apply to all foreign companies “under all circumstances”; its applicability is limited. **So, point II is not correct.**
● Following representations from global investors and after rulings from the A.P. Shah Committee (2015) and clarifications by the CBDT (Central Board of Direct Taxes), **the government exempted Foreign** **Institutional Investors (FIIs) and Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) from the applicability of MAT** on income arising from capital gains on transactions in securities. This exemption aimed to promote foreign investment in Indian markets and avoid double taxation, as FIIs are already liable to tax under specific provisions for capital gains. **So, point III is not correct.**
● MAT is applicable to all companies, including foreign companies. However, it does not apply to companies that have been granted **exemption under Section 10AA of the Income Tax Act, which** **pertains to Special Economic Zones (SEZs). So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In recent years, the government has gradually reduced the MAT rate (from 18.5% to 15%) to align with broader corporate tax reforms and promote business competitiveness.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 States in India are responsible for nearly
two-thirds of the total public expenditure but generate only about one-third of the total revenue.
II. 	 The Fiscal Health Index (FHI) is an initiative
of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) to assess and compare the fiscal performance of States in India.
III. 	West Bengal ranks highest in the Fiscal
Health Index owing to its low fiscal deficit and robust debt profile.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The fiscal health of Indian States is a critical aspect of India’s overall macroeconomic stability. Since States undertake substantial responsibilities in social sector spending and infrastructure development, their ability to manage expenditure, revenue, and debt effectively determines the sustainability of the country’s fiscal system.
● The fiscal structure in India is characterised by a vertical imbalance between the Union and State Governments. **While the States undertake around two-thirds of total public spending (particularly** **in sectors like health, education, rural development, and infrastructure), they raise only about one-** **third of the total revenues**. This imbalance is addressed through fiscal transfers from the Centre — including tax devolution as recommended by the Finance Commission and grants-in-aid. Hence, the statement correctly reflects the expenditure-revenue asymmetry of Indian fiscal federalism. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● **The Fiscal Health Index (FHI), an analytical tool, is an initiative of NITI Aayog as part of its annual** **Study of State Finances.** It uses multiple parameters like debt-GSDP ratio, interest payments to revenue receipts, revenue deficit, fiscal deficit, and primary deficit to gauge the fiscal position of each State. The
purpose of the index is to provide an objective basis for understanding fiscal sustainability and alert policymakers to potential risks of fiscal stress. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● According to the latest RBI report on State Finances, **Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Gujarat are among** **the fiscally healthiest States, while Punjab, Kerala, and West Bengal are not** due to elevated debt burdens and persistent deficits. West Bengal’s debt-GSDP ratio remains above the recommended limits, and its fiscal deficit is relatively high. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Fiscal Health Index 2025 offers a valuable tool for assessing the fiscal performance of Indian States.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the filing of Income Tax Returns
(ITR) under the Income Tax Act of 1961, consider the following statements:
I. 	 An individual below 60 years of age earning
a total income of ₹2.5 lakh or more in a financial year is required to file an ITR.
II. 	 It is used by banks as an important parameter
for assessing the creditworthiness of individuals.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Income Tax Return (ITR) is a formal statement filed by individuals, Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs), and other entities to the Income Tax Department of India under the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961. It declares total income, tax liability, and tax payments made during a financial year.
● Under Section 139(1) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, every individual whose gross total income exceeds the basic exemption limit is required to file an ITR. **For individuals below 60 years of age, the** **exemption limit is ₹2.5 lakh;** for senior citizens (aged 60 to below 80 years), it is ₹3 lakh, and for super senior citizens (aged 80 years or above), it is ₹5 lakh. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Although not a legal requirement, regular ITR filing acts as a formal record of one’s income stability and tax compliance, which enhances financial credibility. Financial institutions, including banks and NBFCs, often require ITR receipts while processing loan applications, credit cards, or visa applications as proof of income. **It demonstrates financial discipline and transparency, both of which are important** **parameters for assessing creditworthiness**. Even salaried individuals with no tax liability benefit from consistent ITR filing for future financial dealings. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) recently released data highlighting a significant increase in ITR filings for the Assessment Year 2024–25, crossing over 8 crore filings before the July 31 deadline.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
‘Deficit financing’ in the Indian context primarily
refers to:

options.a:
Borrowing from the International Monetary
Fund
options.b:
Borrowing or printing of new currency to
finance excess expenditure
options.d:
Raising funds through the disinvestment of
public sector enterprises

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Deficit financing is the budgetary situation where expenditure is higher than the revenue. It is a practice adopted for financing the excess expenditure with outside resources.
● The expenditure-revenue gap is financed by **either printing currency or through borrowing from** **market.** Thus, it is a method of meeting government deficits through the creation of new money.
● The deficit is the gap caused by the excess of government expenditure over its receipts. The expenditure includes disbursement on revenue as well as on capital account. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The money market helps in the effective implementation of the monetary policy of the central bank.
**Statement II:** Money market deals in financial instruments that are short-term and highly liquid.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
